mainText:
### Trump’s Beijing Visit

Today, President Donald Trump is set to arrive in Beijing for a significant three-day visit with Chinese leader Xi Jinping. This marks the first time a U.S. president has visited China since their last meeting in November 2017, and it is anticipated to be the first of four in-person meetings planned for this year.

The dynamics between the two nations have shifted dramatically over the past eight years. As part of his 2024 campaign, Trump made reducing trade deficits a priority. In February 2025, he increased tariffs on Chinese goods by an additional 10 percent, ostensibly to pressure Beijing regarding fentanyl precursors. This move was followed by a doubling of tariffs just a month later, leading to average effective tariffs of 127.2 percent on Chinese imports and 147.6 percent on U.S. goods by April 2025.

Trump and Xi reached a truce in trade negotiations during their summit in Busan, South Korea, in October 2025. Following that, Trump announced he would travel to Beijing, a meeting that was delayed until now due to the ongoing conflict in Iran.

Experts suggest that this summit is critical for both nations, particularly given the substantial security tensions. Scott Kennedy from the Center for Strategic and International Studies emphasized that these meetings require extensive pre-negotiations to facilitate productive discussions.

Key topics for the summit include establishing a viable trade framework, the potential for Chinese influence over Iran to help end the ongoing war, and discussions surrounding Taiwan.

### Inflation Ticks Up

The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April consumer price index report indicating a 3.8 percent increase in headline inflation compared to April 2025, surpassing March’s increase of 3.3 percent. This is the largest rise since May 2023 and marks the first instance since 2023 where inflation has outpaced wage gains. Rising energy costs significantly contributed to this increase: overall energy prices rose by 18 percent year-on-year, with gasoline prices up 28 percent and fuel oil surging 54 percent. Adjusted for inflation, hourly earnings fell by 0.3 percent from last April.

Additionally, the impact of President Trump’s tariff policies is becoming evident, with the price of canned produce rising 5.7 percent and fresh tomato prices nearly 40 percent higher year-on-year. The Federal Reserve is now less likely to approve another rate cut this year, even as Trump’s nominee for Fed chair, Kevin Warsh, is expected to be confirmed by the Senate today.

### Cost, Success of Iran Operations Scrutinized

Jay Hurst, the Pentagon’s acting comptroller, informed Congress that the estimated cost of the U.S.-Iran war has increased to approximately $29 billion, up from $25 billion. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth indicated that Trump has the necessary authority to resume hostilities without Congressional approval. Recent reports reveal that Iran maintains significant missile capabilities, with access restored to 30 of its 33 launch sites, as well as 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile.

Trump is reportedly growing frustrated with Iranian negotiations and is considering a resumption of hostilities. U.S. and Iranian officials disclosed that Saudi Arabia conducted secret retaliatory strikes against Iran following attacks on its territory.

### Fighting Resumes in Ukraine, Following Three-Day Ceasefire

Following the expiration of a U.S.-mediated ceasefire, Russian forces launched over 200 drones into Ukraine, resulting in at least six fatalities. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky announced strikes on Russian infrastructure deep within their territory. U.S. and Ukrainian negotiators are reportedly working on an agreement for Ukraine to sell military technology to the U.S. and to co-produce drones together.

### Ultra-Orthodox Party Calls for Dissolution of the Knesset

Degel HaTorah, an ultra-Orthodox faction within Israel’s United Torah Judaism alliance, has called for the dissolution of the Knesset after Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu informed them he would not advance legislation exempting ultra-Orthodox Jews from military service. This statement has prompted a vote that, if Netanyahu's government loses, could lead to elections as early as August.
